questions.question: How can you see which teammates are currently working on specific blocks in a page?

options.text: A notification bell icon appears in the top right corner.
options.explanation: Notifications alert you to activity, but they do not show where teammates are clicking or editing.

options.text: Their avatars or profile photos appear next to any block they clicked on or are editing.
options.explanation: Real-time collaboration allows you to see exactly where teammates are working by observing their avatars.

options.text: A sidebar list appears showing the names of everyone viewing the page.
options.explanation: While you can see who is viewing, the specific location of their cursor is indicated by the avatars next to blocks.

options.text: You must click on their name in the comments section to locate them.
options.explanation: Avatars appear dynamically next to blocks as they are being edited, not just in the comments.

questions.question: What happens if you and a teammate edit the exact same block at the same time?

options.text: The document locks, and you must wait for them to finish before making changes.
options.explanation: Unlike traditional document software, Notion does not lock content when someone else is editing. You can work simultaneously.

options.text: The most recent change will be reflected on the page.
options.explanation: Notion handles simultaneous edits by showing the most recent change made to the block.

options.text: The edits are merged into a new block automatically.
options.explanation: Edits are not merged into new blocks; they happen directly on the existing block.

options.text: You will receive a conflict resolution error message.
options.explanation: Notion is designed to allow real-time collaboration without requiring manual conflict resolution.

questions.question: How do you quickly jump to the specific location where a teammate is reading or editing?

options.text: Select their name from the 'Share' menu at the top right.
options.explanation: The Share menu is for managing permissions, not locating cursors.

options.text: Refresh the page to reload the collaborative cursor positions.
options.explanation: Refreshing the page does not move you to their location; it simply reloads the document state.

options.text: Click on their avatar at the top of the page.
options.explanation: Clicking a teammate's avatar allows you to jump directly to wherever they are reading or editing.

options.text: Use the keyboard shortcut 'Cmd+K' and type their name.
options.explanation: The Cmd+K shortcut is for the command menu, not for jumping to a teammate's location.

questions.question: How do you lock a page to prevent accidental edits?

options.text: Go to the Share menu and select 'Lock Page'.
options.explanation: The Share menu is used to manage access permissions for specific people, not to lock the page itself.

options.text: Click the page title and toggle the 'Locked' switch in the breadcrumb menu.
options.explanation: You must use the three-dot menu (•••) to toggle the lock on.

options.text: Click ••• at the top of your page and toggle on 'Lock page'.
options.explanation: The three-dot menu provides access to the 'Lock page' toggle, which instantly makes the page read-only.

options.text: Navigate to the database settings and enable 'Read-only mode'.
options.explanation: Page locking is a specific setting found in the page header menu, not the database settings.

questions.question: What is the difference between locking a page and locking a database view?

options.text: Locking a page allows data editing, while locking a database prevents it.
options.explanation: Locking a page makes the entire page read-only, while locking a database locks the structure (views/properties) but allows data editing.

options.text: Locking a page is permanent, but locking a database is temporary.
options.explanation: Both locks can be toggled on and off at any time.

options.text: Locking a page prevents all editing, while locking a database prevents editing of views and properties only.
options.explanation: This accurately describes the function: a page lock is a full read-only state, while a database lock protects the structure.

options.text: You cannot lock a database, only a page.
options.explanation: The article explicitly describes how to lock a database view to protect structure while allowing data entry.

questions.question: Can a member with 'edit' access to a page override a page lock?

options.text: No, the lock applies to everyone regardless of access level.
options.explanation: The lock is designed to be an extra layer of protection that requires intent to bypass, but anyone with edit access can turn it off.

options.text: Yes, they can turn it off using the same ••• menu used to lock it.
options.explanation: Anyone with full or edit access can turn the page lock off using the ••• menu, provided they have edit permissions on the page.

options.text: Only the owner of the workspace can turn off a page lock.
options.explanation: The article specifies that anyone with 'full or edit access' can turn the lock off.

options.text: Yes, but only if they are currently viewing the page at the same time as the lock was applied.
options.explanation: Access permissions determine the ability to unlock, not the timing of the action.

questions.question: What happens when you select 'Unlock for me' on a locked page?

options.text: The page becomes editable for everyone on the team.
options.Explanation: This would be 'Unlock for everyone', which is a separate option in the menu.

options.text: You can make changes, but the page remains locked for others.
options.explanation: Selecting 'Unlock for me' grants you edit access while keeping the lock active for the rest of the workspace.

options.text: The lock icon disappears from the breadcrumb menu permanently.
options.explanation: The lock remains active for others even if you unlock it for yourself.

options.text: You must ask the owner to re-lock it for you to see the lock icon again.
options.explanation: The article notes you can quickly re-lock it yourself using the 'Re-lock' option.

questions.question: If you unlock a database for everyone, how do you re-lock it?

options.text: Select 'Re-lock' in the breadcrumb menu.
options.explanation: The 'Re-lock' option is specifically mentioned for when you unlock a page only for yourself.

options.text: You cannot re-lock a database once you have unlocked it for everyone.
options.explanation: You can re-lock it by returning to the ••• menu and toggling 'Lock database' back on.

options.text: Go to the ••• menu at the top of the page and toggle on 'Lock database'.
options.explanation: To lock the database again after unlocking for everyone, you must use the ••• menu toggle.

options.text: Refresh the page to reset the database to its locked state.
options.explanation: Refreshing the page does not revert a deliberate unlock action.

questions.question: How does Notion indicate that a database has been locked?

options.text: A red lock icon appears next to the database icon in the sidebar.
options.explanation: The lock status is indicated in the breadcrumb menu, not the sidebar.

options.text: The breadcrumb menu displays 'Locked' next to its title.
options.explanation: When a database is locked, the 'Locked' status appears in the top breadcrumb menu next to the title.

options.text: All property names turn gray and cannot be clicked.
options.explanation: While properties might be locked, the visual indicator in the breadcrumb is the authoritative 'Locked' status.

options.text: The database icon changes to a closed folder.
options.explanation: The article does not mention changes to the icon itself, only the breadcrumb text.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'Lock database' feature in full page databases?

options.text: To prevent anyone from viewing the database data.
options.explanation: The purpose is to protect the structure (views and properties) while still allowing data editing.

options.text: To force all users to edit data in a specific format.
options.explanation: Locking the database controls access to structure, not the formatting of data entry.

options.text: To lock the structure (views/properties) while allowing other members to continue editing the actual data.
options.explanation: This allows teams to agree on a specific view or property layout without worrying about accidental changes to those settings.

options.text: To automatically sync the database with external spreadsheets.
options.explanation: Locking is a permission/access feature, not a data integration feature.
